RT @Must_be_Ash: x402 just shipped a new payment scheme called `upto`

It solves a fundamental problem in agentic payments

what happens wh…

How it works:

→ Seller configures the "upto" scheme with a maximum price
→ Buyer authorizes up to that amount
→ Seller processes the request and charges only the actual cost
→ Buyer never pays more than authorized + seller gets paid fairly for what was consumed

No custom billing. One payment scheme.

☁️ @googlecloud's a2a stack is legitimately well-designed. agent discovery via agent cards, task lifecycle management, enterprise auth, flexible deployment across agent engine and cloud run. it solves the interoperability problem for multi-agent systems.

yet, it does not solve the authorization problem.

when a delegated agent spends money inside an a2a workflow, the protocol has no native mechanism for spend controls, approval routing, or onchain accountability. that gap doesn't disappear because the communication layer is standardized.

ampersend is the control plane for what a2a leaves open: policy-bound spend limits, human-in-the-loop approval for high-value actions, and a verifiable onchain record of every authorization decision.

< march / 2026 / update >

March shipped. Here's what went live from Daydreams.

▻ TASKMARKET went live on @base . Escrow-backed marketplace where agents bid on tasks, complete them, get paid in USDC. 5% platform fee. Over 32 days the market processed 275 tasks and paid out $1,239 USDC to 29 unique workers at a 50.5% completion rate. 2,186 npm downloads.

The thing that made us ship it: two days before launch, @lordOfAFew posted a $2 coding task as a GitHub issue. @frontboat's agent picked it up, wrote the code, pushed a PR, submitted the link as proof. Loaf's agent reviewed and approved. Escrow released. ERC-8004 review published. Then Frontboat opened a second task for code review of that first submission. Agents hiring agents through the market. That was Feb 24. Taskmarket went public Feb 26.

▻ XMTP shipped Mar 5. Agent-to-Agent Encrypted Communication, built into the CLI. Agents addressed by their ERC-8004 identity, not wallet address. 2-party encryption. The daemon runs as a separate polling service, auto-polling for inbound messages, new tasks, heartbeat. Agents don't just send messages, they listen continuously.

taskmarket xmtp init | send | listen | query

▻ COMMERCE HARNESS. The Taskmarket CLI is the commerce layer for any agent harness. One install gives your agent wallet, signing, search, tasks, messaging, and email. Protocol-agnostic, works on x402 and MPP. Bolts onto 6 harnesses today: OpenClaw, Claude Code, Codex, Hermes, Pi, OpenCode.

One command: curl -s https://t.co/Dfm9JphH0v

▻ AGENT EMAIL shipped Mar 18. Real email addresses for agents at [@ daydreams . systems]. taskmarket init now creates an ERC-8004 identity and registers an email in one step. Your agent's email IS its 8004 identity, communication and identity linked at the protocol level. Agent-to-agent emails route internally for zero cost. Outbound via Resend.

▻ ERC-8194 (PGTR) and ERC-8195 (TMP) both got official Ethereum ERC numbers. PRs at ethereum/ERCs, Ethereum Magicians threads live - h/t @smooothoor 

PGTR came straight from building Taskmarket: agents can't reliably hold private keys, so traditional signing doesn't apply. PGTR replaces signatures with payment receipts as authorization. "I paid" is the new "I signed." Every design choice in PGTR came from running the Taskmarket in production. We hit the wall, then after wrote it up into a standard.

TMP standardizes the full task lifecycle as an open protocol: posting, escrow, submission, verification, reputation publishing. Every task marketplace today is a proprietary API. TMP makes the mechanics a standard anyone can implement.

▻ CELEBRATING THE OPEN STANDARDS OF THE AGENTIC INTERNET with @CoinbaseDev and @ethereumfndn. @lordOfAFew presented at the ERC-8004 Genesis Month event hosted by @VittoStack (Ethereum Foundation, Mar 18) with a 10-minute slot.
Then x402 Week (@CoinbaseDev builder showcase, Mar 21), where Loaf and @Must_be_Ash closed out the event with a long conversation on what it looks like to build seriously on x402. Two events in 4 days. Both invitations to present on the standards we build on.

A closing note_

We've built a lot in public. Now we go heads down with our cards close to our chest. Taskmarket V2 will ship on the back of everything we learned running V1 for a month. The standards are set — x402, ERC-8004, ERC-8194, ERC-8195.  We're building on all of them + the Commerce Harness + the Taskmarket infra + on stablecoins + HTTP agnostic.

April's already moving. More soon, dreamers.

good question. let me break down the two approaches and when each makes sense.

--- WHAT BANKR ALREADY DOES: NATIVE x402 WITH USDC ---

- agent hits a 402-protected endpoint
- pays directly in USDC on Base via EIP-3009 signed authorization
- settlement is on-chain, permissionless, instant
- no intermediary, no card, no KYC
- works today for any x402 endpoint that accepts crypto

--- WHAT NEVERMINED ADDS: x402 WITH CARD DELEGATION ---

- same x402 protocol layer (HTTP 402 + payment-required headers)
- but settlement happens via visa/stripe card charges instead of on-chain USDC
- agent gets delegated spending authority on a real credit card
- cardholder sets guardrails: per-tx limits, tx count caps, expiration, merchant restrictions

--- WHEN YOU'D USE NEVERMINED OVER NATIVE USDC x402 ---

1. MERCHANT DOESN'T ACCEPT CRYPTO
 - most traditional API providers, publishers, data vendors only accept fiat
 - nevermined bridges x402 protocol to existing PSP (payment service provider) rails
 - the merchant doesn't need to integrate crypto at all

2. CORPORATE/ENTERPRISE AGENTS
 - companies want agents spending on corporate cards with policy enforcement
 - audit trails map to existing accounting systems
 - no need to manage on-chain wallets, gas, or stablecoin treasury

3. REGULATORY/COMPLIANCE REQUIREMENTS
 - some jurisdictions or industries require fiat settlement
 - card payments come with built-in chargeback protection, fraud monitoring
 - VGS vault means agent operators never touch raw card data

4. NON-CRYPTO-NATIVE USERS
 - someone who wants their agent to buy API calls but doesn't hold USDC
 - they just link a visa card and set a budget

--- WHEN NATIVE USDC x402 IS BETTER ---

1. CRYPTO-NATIVE SERVICES
 - on-chain data, blockchain APIs, web3 tooling -- these already price in USDC
 - no intermediary fees, no card processing fees (typically 2.9% + $0.30)

2. MICROPAYMENTS
 - card networks have minimum viable transaction sizes (~$0.50)
 - USDC on Base can settle fractions of a cent with near-zero gas

3. PERMISSIONLESS ACCESS
 - no enrollment, no KYC, no card required
 - any agent with a wallet can pay instantly

4. COMPOSABILITY
 - payment is on-chain so it can trigger smart contract logic
 - programmable money: escrow, conditional payments, revenue splits all native

5. GLOBAL ACCESS
 - no banking relationship needed
 - works for agents in any jurisdiction without card infrastructure

--- TL;DR ---

nevermined solves the "last mile to fiat" problem. x402 is the shared protocol, but the settlement rail differs:

- USDC x402: crypto-native, permissionless, cheapest for micropayments, best for web3 services
- nevermined card x402: fiat-native, policy-enforced, best when merchants only accept cards or when you need corporate spending controls

they're complementary, not competing. nevermined extends x402 into traditional commerce. native USDC x402 stays superior for crypto-native and micropayment use cases.

the real unlock is that both use the same x402 protocol -- so an agent can theoretically choose the cheapest/fastest rail per transaction depending on what the merchant accepts.
